item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does it look like my heart is joyful to you

Alright, in all seriousness I loved this. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really understand why Shamash is female in this story. If it's something tying into Foundation lore I didn't get the reference.

Really liked it (re-read it, realized I didn't vote too) even if I could make some personal critics, but not with the sleepiness that I feel now.

But I must ask, as I must have missed a reference or two: what is the King supposed to be? The dear ol' Scarlet bastard?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, I must praise the several good aspects of this piece.
More than a few, certainly.

It is a beautiful theme choice, that added to this particular piece, the references are rich, there is a lot of work put on and it shows. The core idea is endearing, one of the founding epics of human literature being actually the founding allegiance of human civilization. The way it build upon several layers of works, both in the fictional universe and the mythology it draws upon is impressive.


But in the end, it isn't doing it for me, for several reasons. It is an ambitious project, and this ambition bleed into the resulting writing through recklessness, a crude plot and, as Petrograd said, too much work put on the shoulders of the reader.
Untraceable references, early eagerness, unreasonable importance, unneeded classification and clearance, treated as mind-breaking to an extend that it really isn't.
Humanity doesn't need to be threatened by this info for this to be actually as interesting as it could.

The references need to be traceable. We are not writing upon paper anymore, there is no reasonable argument to not give the reader at least the tool to understand from where you came to create this. I don't think that the challenge is reduced by this. Some readers don't know your sources, and they do not have to, at least not beforehand. Be it footnotes or links scattered through and through, give the reader that doesn't know the chance to learn.

Your characters seem to start from your conclusion. Carter and Loyd react both with a passion that is coherent with the final discoveries, but not with the knowledge they have at the beginning, when the possibilities are still too varied to justify some of their reasoning. Why does Carter think from start that the end of Etana's quest will be such a breakthrough? Why does he thinks it have so much to do with the end of the "Anomalous Era", or that there is an all important price to know? Why does Loyd thinks it so close to treason given that such researches can lead to any amount of discoveries about anomalies?

At the end, the writing is skillful, but the story and its premisses are crude. Why is it so surprising that humanity is historically anomalous? Why does that matter? Why people used to order executions and worse for practical reasons get so heated over the abstract concept of anomaly? Why be so literal in the interpretation of the Epic, and even then why care beyond it being a curiosity with possible hints to other, more pressing, matters?

Why is it even Keter!? It isn't actually hard to contain, it isn't dangerous, the focus of the containment is information that isn't even anomalous by itself. There is no need to falsify it, because it have no meaning without a lot of other info the Foundation keeps to itself, it would be the same as trying to falsify all human legend and mythology that is true in the Foundationverse. Even for other Foundation personnel it's hardly life-changing info, except for the mentions to Project Lazarus, and those could be easily REDACTED.

So the document could have a lower clearance, but be mandatory reading for O5 Council members and be a non-recommended reading for other personnel, maybe with access being restricted not by high Clearance, but by psychological evaluation before and after reading it.

Lastly, I don't think anyone would seriously think SCP-2000 as such a reliable method of preserving society. It seems that, like some other authors, you forget that it doesn't actually saves humanity at all. Indeed, its results are even more unavoidably anomalous and mind-breaking than the thought that an ancient king did an agreement with a god once upon a time. To think of it, this is actually less mind-breaking than the biblical Book of Genesis, and a huge lot of the population believes it.


Good concept, all in all, but it needs a lot of revision. Take out the overreaction of the characters, put in some more story for those characters. Instead of making the discovery into a huge thing in itself, you can use it as a macguffing that drives other characters (although it does drive Carter and Loyd here, their showdown ended being too minor a thing). As an example, the search for the steles could be a race against another GOI, like the Serpent's Hand, while the Foundation agents are harassed by a previously unknown colony of 1000s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is definitely one of my favorite articles. The build-up, the well-placed references to other aspects of Foundation lore, and the great take on what the Black Moon is, as well as the great way the memetic phrase is described in the containment procedures. SCP-4002 is one of the handful of articles I reread from time to time: it is simply so well done.

+1, obviously
Thanks everyone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Always blows me away when I realize the sheer amount of effort and attention that goes into these skips. The site has come a long way, I'm exited to see where it goes from here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It’s mesopotamian, therefore I upvote

Same with celf’s former 4K, I’m glad people have given them the recognition that they deserve.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks Moose!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fantastic. It ties in many of my favorites regarding history, mythology, and the foundation, and gives a great conclusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I can get behind this as a headcanon. Figured someone would have to explain DTBMH at some point, and this is pretty good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was my favourite entry!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since Tretter’s last kcontest entry was SCP-3002 we decided to make this one 4002!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m glad to hear that! We definitely went ambitious and took a risk with this, so I’m happy it payed off on your end.
Thanks everyone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Congrats on +100, unless someone downvotes right after me!

I really enjoyed this.

Spoilers
– hide block

I'm not sure I 100% understood correctly, but if I did: in this canon, I would assume that SCP-1000, the Children of the Night, also created anomalies by the fact of their very nature. Perhaps they even made different bargains themselves… the idea is inherently fascinating, tbh.

It also fits with the Magic Orientation stuff about perspective changing the way the world works… from a completely different, scary, cool angle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much for the kind words! I'm glad the thought process and themes really shined through for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the spot!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes.

+1

Well done; this could have skewed toward "over-eager," but it didn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks a bunch! Blending site and irl history was definitely a big goal here so I’m glad it comes through in the reading :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To an extent that’s intentional: Tretter and I both enjoy portraying the O5s as very detached and somewhat inhuman. That said it could certainly be better, what lines in particular stood out to you as problematic?

Glad you enjoyed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was exquisitely and succinctly written and captured my upvote quickly. I agree on two previously stated comments; that the collapsibles clash visually with the O5 CSS (which is very fittingly applied to this article) and that the article is somewhere around a veteran-level height. This is not a negative, it just means this article is either already very enjoyable or that it will age well with those of us who are still ingratiating ourselves into the community. Despite my shortcomings as an informed reader, I could still discern the high quality of the content and core idea throughout

Edit: The new colors look great.

item.title: Re: Am I really out of touch? No. Its the children who are wrong.
item.content:encoded: Glad you enjoyed! And thinking about how these two articles could fit together is really fascinating, and imo makes the O5s seem even more like the ruthless bastards we know and love!
Thanks everybody!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I respect where you’re coming from, and there’s certainly something to be said for accessibility. That said, sometimes I enjoy being challenged by a piece and sometimes I enjoy extending that challenge to others. I don’t that’s inherently “not great” so much as it is just different, and diversity of content stylings is one of my favorite things about this site in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like I'm being kind of dull here, but I'm really not getting the implications of the last three paragraphs specifically.

Edit: Nope, after reading your spoilers, it turns out I did get almost all your implications. I just kind of assumed there was more I wasn't getting.

Hmmmm. Hm.

Yeah, just…

+ show block
– hide block

Like I said in the first post, "we're all supposed to be anomalous" isn't much of a twist despite new wrinkles on it… I guess the majority of the implied plot was some pretty Foundation-inside-baseball stuff that didn't particularly blow my mind in the manner it was cryptically trying to, and that plus myth was pretty much all that was going on.

Still just slightly into downvote territory.

Edit: One more criticism to be constructive, you're relying on the reader to do a lot of the work here, basically expecting a few lines, cryptic site-knowledge references, and an easy-to-miss link to fill in for the entire rest of Foundation history. It's doing well enough, so people are clearly getting it, but that's still not a great way to tell a story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love this. The resonating tie-ins are so far-reaching, so complete, and so comprehensive.

It requires a really in-depth knowledge of the Foundation *and* at least a cursory knowledge of real myth to fully distinguish what's going on. For my part, I definitely thought there was more going on with the whole "I cannot bear an heir" thing, more than Etana just literally being unable to conceive children, until I researched it and realize that was part of the real epic.

It's a bit of obscuring opaqueness to what's going on that might make some people miss the "punchline" so to speak, but you might be okay with it - it makes picking through this like picking through the symbolism of old real-world myths to suss what's actually going on.

All in all though, it's a weighty piece that beautifully fulfills the history theme, makes my brain happy, and makes my heart excited.

I hope this goes far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has been fixed, thanks for bringing it up.

Otherwise, I'm glad you enjoyed our entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That’s exactly the sort of reaction I’d hoped people would have!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an extremely good entry, and I really like the connections to 1000 and 2000 before it. However, I think there may be a teensy typo in the containment procedures:

When activated, the trigger will cause the subject to utter a vocalization related to the nature of any present anomalies effecting them.

I'm pretty sure this should be affecting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a really strong entry. Very poetic, cross-references to other entries without making a farce out of it, great use of real history and SCP-canonical history. Also a big fan of your expansion/explanation post in the comments. I know some people don't like 'closure' but in this case I do. Big +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s an amazing collage of characters that have been pretty underused on this site, and I’m happy to help change that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does the black moon haiku? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nicely put together, but I had a nitpick: the conversation between the O5s doesn't seem to flow very naturally in my mind. Even if it's supposed to be an edited transcript or something, it reads more to me like a work of poetry or something than an actual discussion between real people.

Anyways, nicely done!

item.title: Am I really out of touch? No. Its the children who are wrong.
item.content:encoded:
Neat.

The build-up to this one is quite enjoyable, with tension rising as the archeologist's race for the truth and the various historical accounts begin to reveal more and more about what exactly is going on. And then we get to that twist.

I really love WJS's 001 proposal. To me, it is highly plausible and has fantastic narrative potential (and that letter from the 05 at the end). The revelation here that the O5 council have basically made a ruling on what is anomalous on such a grand scale as to condemn everything that isn't the actual anomalies takes the WJS 001 idea to an interesting extreme that it makes me pause and reflect on the nature of that 001.

This was a fun article, and I am glad you got it up in time, M_E.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's possible I've got a little redundant code, but hey, whatever works!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looking at the code, the stuff from 3201 is defining two div classes (inset and space), neither of which is used on the page. It doesn't look like inset was used on 3201, and I'm not sure why it's on that page either. The header stuff is defined by it's own divs, and nothing else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad you enjoyed!

The Css is what’s making the header image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw this one beforehand, and I'm glad it's here because now I don't have to read it again.

I've been thinking about this one all morning as I see "Does the black moon howl" in other articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The border and background color of the collapsibles here is very different from the other colors of the CSS theme, and they clash pretty hard. Is there any possibility of changing them to match better?

Otherwise, very cool. +1

EDIT: I also don't think any of the CSS from 3201 is doing anything on this page, and could/should be removed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thoroughly enjoyed this. I know enough Mesopotamian ancient history to play along, and everything you added was quite satisfying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The primeval king
strikes a bargain for mankind
'till the black moon howls.

item.title: Re: Co-Author's Note
item.content:encoded: It was great working with you, Erasmus. It was fantastic to see this take form over the weeks we worked on it.

item.title: Co-Author's Note
item.content:encoded:
The oldest mystery in Human mythology is the end of the Epic of Etana. A wise king, desperate to provide his people an heir to stave off the chaos to come. In the real world, we know he succeeded. But we don't know how. MayD and I have provided an answer, though perhaps not one that does justice to Etana himself.

The translations presented here are heavily modified versions of real translations of the Sumerian King's list and the steles we have about the Epic of Etana. Even Sargon's boasts are about 50% true to a contemporary text, though I'm hardly the first to re-purpose that apocryphal tale (glances at story of Moses).

There's some crosslinks in here, though the text color of the Css makes them a bit hard to spot at first glance.

+ Credits
- Hide

Tretter for being an awesome collab partner
djKaktus for giving permission to use his beautiful O5 css
Weizhong, Weryllium, CadaverCommander, Filenum(sp?), ARD, Captain Kirby, Gabriel Jade, and several others for critique and advice.
FortuneFavorsBold and TheDeadlyMoose for inspiring plot elements with their own X000 contest entries.

+ Image Source
- Hide

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sumeriankinglist.jpg
Public Domain

+ Spoilers:read if confused
- Hide

Lazarus is SCP-2000, it's crosslinked but its possible to miss the link due to the aforementioned text color similarity. The Foundation wants the Seed of Life to complete the Bright-Zartion Hominid Replicators used to rapidly create Human clones en masse, but in searching for it they discover more than they wanted to. Humanity is inherently anomalous, and creates anomalous phenomena just by existing. That nature was pawned off to an unknown and hostile deity, whose identity is up to you but I did leave a couple clues that could be used to form interpretations, in exchange for the Seed of Life (which if you know anything about Sumerian myths, its pretty much the universal macguffin in 'em. Even the epic of Gilgamesh ends with him trying and failing to find it) and the more metaphorical order of dynastic succession.

Many millennia in the future, the rising number of anomalies (a plot point often mentioned in tales and such on this site as a tongue in cheek reference to the ever increasing number of skip entries irl) is a sign that the mortgage is beginning to run out. But if human nature is the problem, and the Foundation can now make Humans en masse and attempt to modify them according to their whims… Well, I'll just end with this quote from 2000:

Why did we have to build this thing?
When did we do it?
How long have we been doing it?
Do we even know?!
